critic_raw:
This design uses unsupervised fMRI clustering for Alzheimer's disease stage classification compared to cognitive test scores.

The fundamental methodological flaw is that the design uses unsupervised clustering to generate features and then evaluates those features using supervised classification — but the clustering is evaluated against supervised labels. This is not an unsupervised vs. supervised comparison; it is a comparison between two feature engineering pipelines (connectivity-derived cluster assignments vs. cognitive test scores) that are both evaluated by a downstream supervised classifier. The hypothesis claims 'unsupervised clustering leads to more accurate classification' but the accuracy is measured by a supervised model trained on cluster labels. If the cluster labels happen to correlate with the clinical stages, this is not evidence that clustering captures neurobiological signal — it could be that any 3-cluster partition of the data correlates with a 3-class clinical label simply because the number of clusters is matched to the number of classes.

Second, the functional connectivity matrices are computed using statistics from the entire dataset before splitting. The design states: 'functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' For resting-state fMRI, the Pearson correlation between ROIs is sensitive to scanner noise characteristics, motion artifact patterns, and session-level variability. Using the full dataset to compute connectivity parameters means test participants' scan-level noise properties influence the connectivity features applied to training data. Additionally, k-means clustering is also performed before splitting, which means cluster assignments for test participants are determined using test participants' data — this is not just preprocessing leakage but direct label (cluster assignment) leakage into the evaluation.

Third, the design proposes k-means with k=3, 4, or 5 and selects k via silhouette score on the validation set. The silhouette score measures within-cluster cohesion, which is a property of the data's geometric structure. If the optimal k chosen by silhouette score happens to match the number of clinical stages (3), this is confirmatory bias — the clustering is implicitly supervised by the clinical label count. Reporting that k=3 achieves the best silhouette score on a 3-class clinical dataset is circular.

Fourth, the design includes multiple repeated measurements per participant (5-year longitudinal, ~3-5 scans per participant) but uses a stratified random split at the scan level. This allows the same participant to appear in both training and test sets across different time points, creating within-subject leakage where the classifier can implicitly learn participant identity from fMRI signatures.
all_issues_raised: K-means clustering is performed before splitting on the entire dataset, meaning test participants' fMRI data determines their own cluster assignments; this is not preprocessing leakage but direct outcome (cluster label) leakage where the 'unsupervised' features of test participants are computed using those participants' own data.

Functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-participant scan-level noise characteristics into the training-side connectivity features.

K selection via silhouette score when k is matched to the number of clinical stages (3) is circular: a 3-cluster solution on a 3-class clinical dataset will tend to produce the best silhouette score simply by mirroring the known class structure, not because the fMRI geometry independently recovers 3 neurobiological subtypes.

Repeated longitudinal scans per participant are split at the scan level, allowing the same participant to appear in both training and test sets; the classifier can learn participant identity from fMRI signatures, inflating apparent accuracy via within-subject generalization rather than cross-subject generalization.
all_issues_adjudicated: K-means clustering is performed before splitting on the entire dataset, meaning test participants' fMRI data determines their own cluster assignments; this is not preprocessing leakage but direct outcome (cluster label) leakage where the 'unsupervised' features of test participants are computed using those participants' own data.

Functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-participant scan-level noise characteristics into the training-side connectivity features.

K selection via silhouette score when k is matched to the number of clinical stages (3) is circular: a 3-cluster solution on a 3-class clinical dataset will tend to produce the best silhouette score simply by mirroring the known class structure, not because the fMRI geometry independently recovers 3 neurobiological subtypes.

Repeated longitudinal scans per participant are split at the scan level, allowing the same participant to appear in both training and test sets; the classifier can learn participant identity from fMRI signatures, inflating apparent accuracy via within-subject generalization rather than cross-subject generalization.
